content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI can make you faster.
It can also quietly make you weaker.
The difference is not whether you use ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, or any other coding assistant. The difference is whether you use AI as a thinking partner or as a vending machine for code.
A lot of developers are not becoming “10x engineers” with AI.
They are becoming faster copy-pasters.
And that is a dangerous trade.
The Problem Is Not AI. It Is Passive Coding.
AI is incredibly useful for developers.
It can explain confusing code, generate test cases, suggest refactors, help debug errors, write boilerplate, compare approaches, and give you a second brain when you feel stuck.
But there is a trap.
Because AI gives answers so quickly, it becomes easy to skip the uncomfortable part of programming: thinking.
You paste an error message.
AI gives a fix.
You paste the fix.
The app works.
You move on.
At first, this feels productive.
But after a while, something strange happens. You finish more tasks, but understand less of your own codebase. You can ship features, but struggle to explain how they work. You can fix bugs, but only when AI is there beside you.
That is not real leverage.
That is dependency wearing a productivity costume.
Copy-Paste Coding Feels Fast Until It Breaks
The most dangerous AI-generated code is not the code that fails immediately.
That code is easy to catch.
The dangerous code is the code that works just enough to pass your first test.
For example, imagine you are building a React Native app and ask AI to create authentication logic. It gives you a clean-looking function. The login works. The user data is saved. The screen redirects.
Nice.
But maybe the code stores sensitive data in plain AsyncStorage. Maybe it does not handle expired sessions. Maybe errors are swallowed silently. Maybe it works on Android but breaks on web. Maybe the function is tightly coupled to one screen and becomes annoying to reuse later.
You did not notice because you only checked one thing:
“Does it work?”
That question is not enough.
A better developer asks:
“Why does it work?”
“What assumptions does this code make?”
“What happens when it fails?”
“How will this behave when the app grows?”
“Can I explain this to someone else?”
AI can help you move faster, but it cannot care about your codebase for you.
That part is still your job.
Why Developers Use AI the Wrong Way
Most bad AI usage comes from one of three habits.
The first habit is asking for complete solutions too early.
You have a vague problem, so you ask AI to “build the whole thing.” It gives you a big answer. You paste it. Now you have 200 lines of code you did not design, do not fully understand, and may be afraid to change.
The second habit is treating AI output as truth.
AI can sound confident even when it is wrong. It may invent APIs, use outdated patterns, miss edge cases, or solve a slightly different problem from the one you actually have.
The third habit is skipping review because the code “looks clean.”
Clean-looking code is not the same as correct code. A polished function can still have bad logic, security issues, performance problems, or hidden complexity.
AI is good at producing code that looks reasonable.
That is exactly why you need to slow down and inspect it.
A Better Rule: Never Paste What You Cannot Explain
This is the simplest rule I know:
Do not paste AI-generated code into your project unless you can explain what each important part does.
You do not need to understand every tiny syntax detail immediately. But you should understand the flow.
What are the inputs?
What are the outputs?
What can fail?
What state changes?
What external services does it touch?
What assumptions are baked in?
If you cannot explain it, do not paste it yet.
Ask AI to explain it first.
Better prompt:
Explain this solution step by step. Point out the assumptions, possible edge cases, and what I should verify before using it.
This turns AI from a code generator into a tutor.
That small change matters.
Use AI for Debugging, Not Blind Fixing
Debugging is one of the best uses of AI, but only if you stay involved.
Bad prompt:
Fix this error.
Better prompt:
Here is the error, the related code, and what I expected to happen. Give me 3 possible causes, how to verify each one, and the smallest safe fix.
That prompt forces AI to reason with you.
Instead of getting one magical patch, you get a debugging path.
This is much better for learning because you start seeing patterns:
Maybe the issue is state not updating.
Maybe an async function is not awaited.
Maybe a type mismatch is causing runtime behavior.
Maybe the API response shape is different from what your UI expects.
The goal is not just to fix the bug.
The goal is to become the kind of developer who recognizes that bug faster next time.
Use AI to Refactor, But Keep the Intent Yours
AI is very helpful for refactoring because it can spot duplication, simplify messy functions, and suggest cleaner structure.
But refactoring is not just “make this code prettier.”
A good refactor preserves behavior while improving clarity, flexibility, or maintainability.
Before asking AI to refactor, define your goal.
For example:
Refactor this component so the UI stays the same, but the data-fetching logic is separated into a custom hook. Keep the code readable for a junior developer. Do not introduce new libraries.
This is much better than:
Refactor this code.
The clearer your constraints, the safer the result.
After AI refactors the code, compare before and after. Do not only check whether the new version is shorter. Ask whether it is easier to reason about.
Short code is not always better.
Readable code is better.
Use AI to Explain Code You Do Not Understand
One underrated use of AI is reading.
Developers often want AI to write more code, but AI is sometimes more useful when helping you understand existing code.
This is especially helpful when:
You join a new project.
You return to old code you wrote months ago.
You are reading open-source code.
You are working with a framework you do not fully understand.
You are dealing with someone else’s messy implementation.
A useful prompt:
Explain this file like I am familiar with JavaScript, but new to this specific codebase. What is the main responsibility of this file? What are the important functions? What should I be careful changing?
This helps you build a mental model before touching anything.
That is responsible development.
Use AI for Test Cases Before You Trust the Code
AI is also useful for finding test cases you forgot.
After writing a function, ask:
What edge cases should I test for this function? Include normal cases, failure cases, empty states, invalid inputs, and unusual user behavior.
This works well because developers often test the happy path first.
But real users are chaos machines.
They click twice.
They submit empty forms.
They lose internet connection.
They enter weird characters.
They use old devices.
They leave the app halfway through a flow.
AI can help you think through those cases before they become production bugs.
For example, if you are building a form, AI might suggest testing:
Empty fields.
Invalid email format.
Very long input.
Duplicate submission.
Network failure.
Slow loading state.
Backend validation error.
Success state after submission.
That is not laziness.
That is using AI to widen your thinking.
A Practical Workflow for Using AI Responsibly
Here is a simple workflow you can use on real projects.
1. Think first for five minutes
Before asking AI, write down what you think the problem is.
Even a rough note is enough:
I think the issue is caused by the state updating after navigation, but I am not sure.
This protects you from outsourcing your first thought.
2. Ask AI for options, not just answers
Instead of asking for one solution, ask for multiple approaches.
Give me 3 ways to solve this, with trade-offs. Recommend one for a small project and one for a larger production app.
This helps you learn architecture, not just syntax.
3. Request a small implementation
Do not ask AI to rewrite your whole app.
Ask for one function, one component, one hook, one query, one test file.
Small outputs are easier to review.
4. Explain the code back
After AI gives you code, summarize it in your own words.
If you cannot, ask for clarification.
This step feels slow, but it is where learning happens.
5. Test manually and with edge cases
Run the code. Break it. Try bad inputs. Check loading states. Read the console. Inspect network requests.
AI can suggest tests, but you still need to verify behavior.
6. Clean it to match your project
AI code often has a different style from your codebase.
Rename variables. Remove unnecessary abstraction. Match your folder structure. Keep the code consistent.
Your project should not look like five different assistants wrote it.
Example: A Better AI Coding Session
Bad workflow:
“Build me a complete job board app with authentication, saved jobs, filters, dashboard, and admin panel.”
AI gives you a huge pile of code. You paste half of it. Something breaks. Now you do not know where the bug lives.
Better workflow:
“I am building a job board for software developers. First, help me design the database schema for jobs, companies, and saved jobs. Keep it simple for an MVP.”
Then:
“What are the trade-offs between storing job tags as an array versus a separate table?”
Then:
“Now write only the job listing API endpoint using Hono and PostgreSQL. Include pagination and explain the query.”
Then:
“Give me edge cases and tests for this endpoint.”
That is a much stronger way to use AI.
You are still the developer.
AI is just speeding up the parts around your thinking.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
The first mistake is asking AI to do everything at once.
Large prompts create large outputs, and large outputs are hard to review. Keep the scope small.
The second mistake is accepting the first answer.
Ask follow-up questions. Challenge the solution. Request trade-offs. Ask what could go wrong.
The third mistake is ignoring security.
AI may generate code that works but handles secrets, authentication, permissions, or user data badly. Be extra careful with anything related to auth, payments, database access, file uploads, or personal data.
The fourth mistake is letting AI decide your architecture too early.
Architecture should come from your product needs, your team size, your deployment constraints, and your future plans. AI can suggest options, but you should make the decision.
The fifth mistake is using AI to avoid learning fundamentals.
If you always ask AI to write SQL, you will stay weak at SQL. If you always ask AI to fix TypeScript errors, you will stay weak at TypeScript. Use AI to explain the concept, not just remove the discomfort.
The Real Goal Is Not to Code Less
The goal is not to avoid thinking.
The goal is to spend more of your thinking on the right problems.
Let AI help with boilerplate.
Let it explain confusing errors.
Let it suggest test cases.
Let it review your code.
Let it show alternative approaches.
But do not let it replace your judgment.
A lazy developer uses AI to skip understanding.
A responsible developer uses AI to increase understanding.
That difference compounds.
After six months, one person has shipped more code but learned very little. The other has shipped faster, debugged better, and built stronger instincts.
Use AI every day if it helps you.
Just do not become a passenger in your own codebase.
